questions.question: Match each motor structure to its primary function:
categories: Specifies what and how to move
Contains lower motor neurons and CPGs

correct_mapping.Motor cortex: Specifies what and how to move
correct_mapping.Spinal cord: Contains lower motor neurons and CPGs

questions.explanation: The motor hierarchy: motor cortex (upper motor neurons) specifies movements, basal ganglia gate when to release them, cerebellum corrects errors by comparing intended vs actual movement, and the spinal cord houses lower motor neurons and CPGs.

questions.question: Match each disorder to its underlying cause:
questions.explanation: Each disorder maps to a specific site of damage: Parkinson's = substantia nigra (dopamine loss), Huntington's = caudate (striatal degeneration), hemiballismus = subthalamic nucleus (loss of indirect pathway excitation), cerebellar ataxia = cerebellum.

questions.question: Match each disorder to its characteristic movement symptom:
questions.explanation: Each motor disorder has a distinctive movement pattern: Parkinson's features tremor at rest, Huntington's shows choreiform movements, hemiballismus involves violent involuntary flinging, and cerebellar damage causes intention tremor during goal-directed movements.

questions.question: Match each brain area to its role in motor planning vs execution:
categories: Direct motor execution and population coding

correct_mapping.Primary motor cortex (M1): Direct motor execution and population coding

questions.explanation: M1 handles direct execution via population coding, premotor cortex plans externally-cued movements, SMA plans self-initiated sequences, and lower motor neurons are the final common pathway to muscles.

questions.question: Match each basal ganglia pathway to its effect:
categories: Provides additional inhibition of unwanted movement
Inhibits until the right time to move

correct_mapping.Indirect pathway: Provides additional inhibition of unwanted movement
correct_mapping.Gating mechanism: Inhibits until the right time to move

questions.explanation: The direct pathway releases movement by disinhibiting the thalamus, the indirect pathway further suppresses unwanted movements, the BG run three parallel circuits (motor, emotional, cognitive), and overall the BG gate movements via tonic inhibition.

questions.question: Match each cerebellar division to its function:
questions.explanation: The three cerebellar divisions serve distinct functions: spinocerebellum coordinates ongoing body movements using spinal input, cerebrocerebellum handles planning and cognition via cortical connections, and vestibulocerebellum maintains balance and controls eye movements.

questions.question: Match each concept to its correct description:
categories: Movement direction by vector sum of neural activity
Body parts mapped to cortical regions
Brain signal preceding conscious urge to move

correct_mapping.Population coding: Movement direction by vector sum of neural activity
correct_mapping.Somatotopic organisation: Body parts mapped to cortical regions
correct_mapping.Readiness potential: Brain signal preceding conscious urge to move

questions.explanation: Population coding (Georgopoulos) uses vector sums, somatotopy maps body parts to cortex, decussation is the crossing at the pyramids for contralateral control, and the readiness potential precedes conscious movement intention.

questions.question: Match each motor learning phase to its brain activation pattern:
categories: Broad cortical activation across many areas
Specific activation in M1 and cerebellum

correct_mapping.Early learning phase: Broad cortical activation across many areas
correct_mapping.Advanced learning phase: Specific activation in M1 and cerebellum

questions.explanation: Motor learning transitions from broad cortical activation (early phase, many areas recruited) to specific, efficient activation focused on primary motor cortex and cerebellum (advanced phase, automatised skill).

questions.question: Match each laterality principle to the correct structure:
questions.explanation: The motor cortex controls the opposite (contralateral) side of the body because axons cross at the medullary pyramids. The cerebellum controls the same (ipsilateral) side — right cerebellar damage affects right-sided movements.

questions.question: Match each key finding to its implication:
categories: Challenges the concept of free will
Higher brain not required for rhythmic movement
Basal ganglia process emotion and cognition too

correct_mapping.Readiness potential precedes conscious w: Challenges the concept of free will
correct_mapping.CPGs function after spinal transection: Higher brain not required for rhythmic movement
correct_mapping.Three parallel BG networks: Basal ganglia process emotion and cognition too

questions.explanation: Each finding broadens our understanding: readiness potential challenges free will, CPGs show spinal autonomy, cerebellar cognition shows motor structures serve broader roles, and parallel BG networks show the basal ganglia process beyond just movement.
